title: ComfyUI Tutorial Series Ep 72: Z-Image Turbo Workflows, ControlNet Essentials & LoRA Training
description:
In this episode, you’ll learn how to set up and use the Z-Image Turbo model in ComfyUI, compare BF16 vs FP8 performance, build ControlNet-based consistency workflows, generate prompts with Qwen VL, and train effective LoRAs for characters and styles.

You’ll see full workflow demonstrations, speed comparisons, prompt techniques, and practical tips for avoiding common issues like oversmoothing, unwanted artifacts, or inaccurate styles. This tutorial is ideal for users who want realistic renders, consistent characters, and optimized settings for image-to-video pipelines.

What You’ll Learn
- How to install, configure, and choose between Z-Image BF16, FP8, and All-in-One models
- Best settings for steps, schedulers, CFG, and resolution
- How image dimensions affect clarity and style
- Using Qwen VL to auto-generate prompts from reference images
- ControlNet basics with Canny, Depth, and DW pose preprocessors
- How to train a LoRA using FAL: steps, trigger words, settings, and troubleshoot
transcript: Welcome to episode 72 of our comfy UI tutorial series. Besides realism, it can also create all kinds of art styles and illustrations.  I will also cover how to use ControlNet to keep things more consistent,  and I will show you how I trained Alora online so I can have a consistent character.  Let's get started.  Z-Image Turbo model, or ZIT, as people call it on social media,  is a 6 billion parameter model.  At the moment of this recording, only the Turbo model is released,  but the base and edit models are coming next, so keep an eye on these.  I have a few workflows for it that you can get for free from Discord.  I am using the BF16 version, but if your video card is not very powerful,  you can try the FP8 version.  In this workflow, you can have both BF16 and FP8 versions as an all-in-one model.  That means the Clip and VAE are included in the checkpoint,  compared with this one that has them split.  I like to use the split version because I learn what a workflow needs,  like what Clip and VAE to use.  But this one produces the same results.  The models are just combined into one.  The FP8 version is smaller, but not always faster.  It depends on your system, and it can have lower quality compared to BF16.  Let me explain how to set it up.  You have everything you need to run the workflow in this PixelRoma note on the left.  Download the Z-Image model and place it in the diffusion folder.  You can click where it says here to download the model.  Download the text encoder.  The quen model is like the brain that reads and understands your prompt,  so the Z-Image model knows exactly what kind of picture to generate. If you have 12GB of VRAM or lower, try the FP8 version,  and if you have 16GB of VRAM or higher, use the BF16 version. I left the FP8 version as default in this workflow.  So let's move to the first workflow and show you what settings I use.  In the load clip node, use the Lumina 2 type because it is the format quen needs,  so the Z-Image model can correctly interpret the prompt embeddings.  For the prompts, you can use long detailed prompts like we use for flux and the quen image model.  For the model shift, I use 3.
